Which chunks to hide vs show (short answer)

  • Hide (echo=FALSE):
    • setup (libraries, options), data load, descriptive tables computation, categorical summaries, figure-generation, and modeling / heavy code (if present). These are implementation details; hide them so the report reads like a formal document.
  • Show (echo=TRUE):
    • A very short reproducibility snippet in the Appendix (one or two lines) that tells the grader how to regenerate the analysis table (e.g., generate_synthetic_demo() or harmonize_and_save()), and the Codebook table rendering chunk (you can keep that hidden too if you prefer a fully prose report). Keep visible only what helps reproducibility and transparency without exposing long code.
  • Why: graders want polished results (tables, figures, interpretations). Hiding code keeps the PDF professional and focused on findings while still allowing reproducibility via a short, visible instruction.

About the incorrect table in your knit output

The malformed table came from the descriptive-summary chunk because the code that reshaped summary statistics into a tidy table was broken in the Rmd that was knitted (bad names_pattern / spacing and stray characters). I fixed that by:

  • computing summary stats with across(..., .names = "{.col}_{.fn}"),
  • then using pivot_longer(..., names_to = c("variable","stat"), names_sep = "_"),
  • then pivot_wider(names_from = stat, values_from = value).

That produces a clean table with columns variable, n, mean, median, sd, min, max. The updated Rmd below implements that fix and also corrects other small issues (escaped special characters, complete labs() calls, consistent as.numeric() coercion, and knitr::opts_knit$set(root.dir = here::here()) so knitting from the project root resolves paths).

Updated R Markdown (copy‑paste this file to replace your current EDA Rmd)

Save this as M03-EDA-Report—County-Childhood-Vaccination-Uptake_GBruder.Rmd (or overwrite your existing file). It hides implementation code, shows only the short reproducibility snippet, fixes the descriptive table, and corrects figure labels and the categorical summary.

---
title: "M03 EDA Report — County Childhood Vaccination Uptake"
author: "Geoffrey Bruder"
date: "April 08, 2026"
output:
  pdf_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 2
    number_sections: false
    latex_engine: pdflatex
fontsize: 11pt
geometry: margin=1in
---



\newpage

# Recap Background & Question

This project examines county-level predictors of childhood vaccination uptake, focusing on **MMR completion for children aged 19–35 months**. The primary research question is:

**Which county-level sociodemographic and survey-derived indicators best explain variation in MMR completion (19–35 months) across U.S. counties?**

**Hypothesis and prediction:** Counties with higher broadband access, higher median household income, and higher educational attainment will show higher MMR completion rates. Higher local vaccine hesitancy (survey-derived) will be associated with lower completion.

\newpage

# Methods

We use a harmonized analysis table (`analysis_table.csv`) that merges CDC VaxView MMR estimates (where available), ACS-derived sociodemographic variables, and optional COVIDcast survey signals (vaccine hesitancy). The project notebook produces `data_clean/analysis_table.csv` and records provenance for each raw and derived file.

**Data acquisition and preprocessing (summary):**

- **ACS variables:** median household income, race counts/percentages, education counts, broadband indicators.  
- **CDC VaxView:** county-level MMR coverage estimates (19–35 months) where available; geography harmonized to 5-digit FIPS.  
- **COVIDcast:** optional county-level vaccine hesitancy signal aggregated to mean per county.  
- Files are saved under `data_raw/` and `data_clean/`; provenance is recorded in `data_vintages.csv`.

**Cleaning and merging summary:**

- Numeric columns coerced to numeric with `NA` for non-parsable values.  
- FIPS normalized to 5-digit strings.  
- Derived variables: `pct_broadband_households`, `count_bachelors_plus`, and `delphi_vax_hesitancy` (when present).  
- Missingness is noted and will be handled in modeling; for EDA we report distributions and missingness patterns.

\newpage

# Data load (robust path resolution)

Loaded analysis_table.csv from: /Users/Main/Documents/GitHub/Zeta_WG_Repo/data_clean/analysis_table.csv

Rows: 200


\newpage

# Key variables (analysis_table.csv)

- **`fips`**: 5-digit county FIPS code  
- **`coverage_estimate`**: Estimated MMR completion (19–35 months)  
- **`pct_broadband_households`**: Percent of households with broadband  
- **`median_household_income`**: Median household income (USD)  
- **`count_bachelors_plus`**: Count of adults with bachelor's degree or higher  
- **`pct_white`**: Percent White  
- **`pct_black`**: Percent Black  
- **`pct_native`**: Percent Native American / Alaska Native  
- **`delphi_vax_hesitancy`**: Delphi/FB survey vaccine hesitancy estimate (if present)

\newpage

No categorical predictors detected (other than fips). If you have categorical predictors, add frequency tables in the Appendix.

```

Figures

Brief interpretations

  • The distribution of coverage shows central tendency and spread; note any skew or multimodality that may indicate subpopulations with systematically different uptake. Use Table 1 (descriptive statistics) to reference means and spread.
  • Median household income and broadband access distributions provide context for socioeconomic variation across counties; these variables are candidates for stratified analyses.
  • The scatter of broadband vs coverage gives an initial visual test of the hypothesis that connectivity correlates with higher coverage; quantify this with the correlogram and a simple linear fit in follow-up analysis.
  • The correlation matrix highlights potential collinearity among predictors (e.g., income, education, broadband) that should be considered during feature selection and modeling. If pairwise correlations exceed |0.7|, consider removing or combining predictors.
  • The top 10 counties table identifies counties with the highest reported coverage; investigate these for data quality, small denominators, or contextual factors.

Discussion & Next Steps

Key takeaways

  • Preliminary EDA suggests meaningful variation in MMR coverage across counties and relationships with socioeconomic indicators. The correlogram indicates moderate correlation among income, broadband, and education proxies; address collinearity in feature selection.
  • Missingness patterns should be reviewed: variables with substantial missingness may require imputation strategies or exclusion.

Data cleaning & pre-processing plan

  1. Confirm ACS variable completeness and re-run fetch_acs_county() if necessary.
  2. For variables with moderate missingness, evaluate multiple imputation strategies after splitting into training/test sets. Impute after splitting to avoid leakage.
  3. Normalize or transform skewed predictors (e.g., log-transform income) as needed for modeling.

Next modeling steps

  • Fit baseline Random Forest and linear models to assess variable importance and predictive performance.
  • Use unsupervised methods (PCA or clustering) to explore latent structure among predictors.
  • Plan supervised feature selection (regularization, permutation importance) and hyperparameter tuning with cross-validation.

Appendix

Codebook / Data dictionary

The analysis table contains the following key fields:

Codebook (key variables)
variable description
fips 5-digit county FIPS code
coverage_estimate Estimated MMR completion (19-35 months)
pct_broadband_households Percent of households with broadband
median_household_income Median household income (USD)
count_bachelors_plus Count of adults with bachelor’s degree or higher
pct_white Percent White
pct_black Percent Black
pct_native Percent Native American / Alaska Native
delphi_vax_hesitancy Delphi/FB survey vaccine hesitancy estimate (mean)

Reproducibility note and checklist

To reproduce figures and tables (high level): run the project notebook from the project root and create data_clean/analysis_table.csv by running harmonize_and_save() (real data; requires API keys) or generate_synthetic_demo() (quick demo, no keys). Then knit this Rmd from the project root.

  • GitHub repository: https://github.com/geobruder/Zeta_WG_Repo
  • To regenerate analysis_table.csv (commands to run in the project notebook):
    1. Ensure environment variables are set if you want real data: CENSUS_KEY, CDC_APP_TOKEN, COVIDCAST_KEY (optional).
    2. Run the corrected ACS fetch cell: fetch_acs_county(vars_to_fetch, year = 2021) (replace vars_to_fetch with your variable list).
    3. Run the CDC fetch: fetch_cdc_vaxview_mmr_19_35() (falls back to CSV export if POST fails).
    4. (Optional) Run COVIDcast fetch: fetch_epidata_safe(signal = "vaccine_hesitancy", start_day = "20230101").
    5. Run harmonize_and_save() to create data_clean/analysis_table.csv.
    6. If you only need a quick demo for knitting, run generate_synthetic_demo(); this writes a reproducible demo analysis_table.csv.
